message.content: Adversaries may exploit the domain controller authentication process to bypass standard mechanisms and gain unauthorized access to accounts. Techniques like Skeleton Key involve patching the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) on a domain controller, allowing attackers to inject false credentials and create a backdoor. This backdoor enables adversaries to authenticate as any domain user until the domain controller is rebooted. Such access can lead to extensive control over hosts and resources, especially in single-factor authentication environments. Notable malware like Chimera has been known to alter NTLM authentication to facilitate unauthorized logins. Mitigation strategies include regular monitoring and auditing of domain controllers, implementing multi-factor authentication, and ensuring timely system reboots to clear any unauthorized patches from memory.
